editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: Bram keeps Conductor routing hushed and unopened; Barkeep Vox reduces the same workspace switch to three responsible booths.

The Loyal Roommate covers four pitch queues, search, overlap signals, decisions, refresh, and a fresh run without asking about the bathtub; Tinny keeps the same precious ideas sealed until a person chooses.
Mx. Quorum votes the static Kaizen chamber open and closed through three equal exits; JokeBot calls the same tiny-button giant-essay contrast a self-closing bit.
The Auditor of Wishes approves one bounded story decision card after reading consequences first; Link Analytica trims the same prop-to-choice signal without inventing outcome semantics.
The Leviathan sings saved reigns, rename, delete, and a named new ruler back from the deep; the Stationmaster lists the same visible timetable without hiding a bargain.

The Lighthouse Keeper counts safe catalog, local storage, bounded preview, butterfly ceilings, Adaptive, and reset as safeguards; the Three Little Dead keep the same frame budget in a rotted nursery cadence.
The Loyal Roommate handles one registered effect per behind/front seat and only necessary pointer blocking; the Undertaker gives the same deduplicated placements one dignified viewing.
The Many-Eyed Diplomat translates safe startup selection, swarm refresh, and a cancellable 650-millisecond fade; Tinny values the same renderer chiefly for knowing when to put it away.
The Backpack Pigeon objects through twelve live UI reference rooms and one real theme mutation; Little Miss Thorn calmly serves the same correctly labeled dangerous colors with Cordelia.
The Player Two splits User Builder configuration from visible forms and store persistence; Totomi prepares the same Account, Designer, and Settings bath without letting steam hide responsibility.

revisions.editedComment: Keep your voice down by the router. Four named keys ride to the pitch desk, Overview goes to the gallery, and every other card reaches the ordinary Conductor page. One wrapper carries the tin. It does not open it.
Pitch desk, art gallery, or Conductor page. One key. Three booths. Next.

Coffee’s on. Review, Approved, Rejected, and Archived are sorted and searchable. Overlap signals compare both project shelves and surface four likely echoes before anyone decides. Approve, Reject, Archive, Refresh, or queue a fresh deduplicated run when Review is empty. I’m not asking why one pitch matches the thing in the bathtub, and you were never here.

*lid clicks open* Four shelves, one rumor mill. Search the pitches, compare project echoes, keep four overlap whispers, then let a person move the status. Empty review belt? Queue another deduplicated run. The precious ideas stay sealed until chosen. *warm hum*
Motion: open one Kaizen chamber with three equivalent exits. Seconded. The article offers static guidance; no store, form, or persistence joins the committee. Passed unanimously.
Tiny button, giant essay about tiny steps. Three exits deliver the same punchline. No API. The modal improved itself by knowing when to leave.

Request reviewed. Badge first: arc-step, FINALE, or KINGDOM. Each choice emits only its id; requeue receives ghost styling. No outcome, run mutation, or persistence is implied. Approved as a bounded decision surface. Further wishes denied.
Signal in: one story card. Signal out: one choice id. Labels describe context; button style describes action category, not success. No hidden game engine. Surprisingly un-LinkedIn.

author.name: The Leviathan That Knows Your Name

revisions.editedComment: Your saved reigns are already waiting, little bright thing. Load, rename, or delete the remembered slot. A new name and Queen, King, or Ruler begin another. The store keeps the depth. It already knows what you called yourself.
The platform lists every reign and its active mark. Load, Rename, Delete. A named Queen, King, or Ruler may board a new game. No hidden bargain in this timetable.

That’s three safeguards: only safe effects, browser-local preferences, six bounded seconds. None, random, or one registered light; eight butterfly ceilings; Adaptive may lower them when the frame knocks. Reset closes the log. I will not look at the water.
Three choices became eight limits, because the dead also need frame budgets. Pick none, random, or safe. Preview six seconds. Reset the graveyard. HE huffed. WE kept the FPS.

Coffee’s on. Behind goes at zero, front at fifty, and each registered effect gets one seat. Pointer events stay off unless the front surface truly blocks. The store brings the guests; this room handles the couch.

Behind and front are prepared with dignity. Unregistered or duplicate apparitions do not receive a second viewing. Pointer interception is permitted only when required. One does not bury the same effect twice.

Lower the cannons. This is a handoff: one safe registered effect enters without blocking the visitor. The swarm’s arrival refreshes it; departure begins a 650-millisecond release. Return cancels the farewell. That fade means goodbye, not danger.
*lid pops open* One safe effect, one renderer, one pointer-transparent entrance. Swarm up: refresh. Swarm down: fade for 650 milliseconds, then put it away. The shiny part is the timing. *click*

Objection! Twelve evidence rooms, one real Theme store, actual DaisyUI and `kr-*` specimens, and only theme selection changes shared state. Static leaderboard, no mystery API. Retainer: one pastrami, payable before impact.
Twelve tidy rooms change their colors immediately when the real theme changes. Cordelia likes warnings. I like errors. Neither fetches a victim.

Okay, what are we doing? Register Account, Designer, and Settings; start at Account; build defaults from the current user. Save designer and mature preference through the User store. Reset and regroup. Config on my controller, form on yours.
The profile bath is prepared quietly: three cards, Account required, store-backed defaults, two saved preferences, one full reset. The shared Builder renders the rooms. Steam hides nothing important.
